volumeTitle: The Woman of Andros. The Self-Tormentor. The Eunuch
label: The Woman of Andros (Section 1.4.230 - Section 2.1.321d)

sections.label: Section 1.4.230
sections.original: Nec satis digna cui committas primo partu mulierem ;
sections.translation: and not sufficiently trustworthy for you to commit to her care a female at her first delivery ; is she still to be brought ? ( She receives an answer from within , and comes forward .) Do look at the inconsiderateness of the old woman ; because she is her pot-companion . Ye Gods , I do entreat you , give her ease in her delivery , and to that woman an opportunity of making her mistakes elsewhere in preference . But why do I see Pamphilus so out of spirits ? I fear what it may be .

sections.label: Section 1.4.235
sections.original: Opperiar ut sciam numquidnam haec turbae tristitia adferat .
sections.translation: I ’ ll wait , that I may know whether this sorrow portends any disaster .

sections.label: Section 1.5.236
sections.original: Hocine est humanum factum aut inceptum ? hocine est officium patris ?
sections.translation: Is it humane to do or to devise this ? Is this the duty of a father ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.237
sections.original: Quid illud est ?
sections.translation: What does this mean ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.237b
sections.original: Pro deum atque hominum fidem , quid est si non haec contumelia est ?
sections.translation: O , by our faith in the Gods ! what is , if this is not , an indignity ? He had resolved that he himself would give me a wife to-day ; ought I not to have known this beforehand ? Ought it not to have been mentioned previously ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.240
sections.original: Miseram me , quod verbum audio ?
sections.translation: Wretched me ! What language do I hear ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.241
sections.original: Quid Chremes qui denegarat se commissurum mihi
sections.translation: What does Chremes do ? He who had declared that he would not intrust his daughter to me as a wife ; because he himself sees me unchanged he has changed . Thus perversely does he lend his aid , that he may withdraw wretched me from Glycerium . If this is effected , I am utterly undone .

sections.label: Section 1.5.245
sections.original: Adeon ’ hominem esse invenustum aut infelicem quemquam ut ego sum ?
sections.translation: That any man should be so unhappy in love , or so unfortunate as I am ! Oh , faith of Gods and men ! shall I by no device be able to escape this alliance with Chremes ? In how many ways am I contemned , and held in scorn ? Every thing done , and concluded ! Alas ! once rejected I am sought again ; for what reason ? Unless perhaps it is this , which I suspect it is :

sections.label: Section 1.5.250
sections.original: Aliquid monstri alunt : ea quoniam nemini obtrudi potest ,
sections.translation: they are rearing some monster , [*] and as she can not be pushed off upon any one else , they have recourse to me .

sections.label: Section 1.5.251b
sections.original: Oratio haec me miseram exanimavit metu .
sections.translation: This language has terrified wretched me with apprehension .

sections.label: Section 1.5.252
sections.original: Nam quid ego nunc dicam de patre ? ah
sections.translation: But what am I to say about my father ? Alas ! that he should so thoughtlessly conclude an affair of such importance ! Passing me in the Forum just now , he said , Pamphilus , you must be married to-day :

sections.label: Section 1.5.255
sections.original: Abi domum . Id mi visus est dicere , Abi cito , et suspende te .
sections.translation: get ready ; be off home . He seemed to me to say this : Be off this instant , and go hang yourself . I was amazed ; think you that I was able to utter a single word , or any excuse , even a frivolous , false , or lame one ? I was speechless . But if any one were to ask me now what I would have done , if I had known this sooner , why , I would have done any thing rather than do this . But now , what course shall I first adopt ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.260
sections.original: Tot me impediunt curae , quae meum animum divorsae trahunt ,
sections.translation: So many cares beset me , which rend my mind to pieces ; love , sympathy for her , the worry of this marriage ; then , respect for my father , who has ever , until now , with such an indulgent disposition , allowed me to do whatever was agreeable to my feelings . Ought I to oppose him ? Ah me ! I am in uncertainty what to do .

sections.label: Section 1.5.264b
sections.original: Misera timeo incertum hoc quorsum accidat .
sections.translation: I ’ m wretchedly afraid how this uncertainty is to terminate .

sections.label: Section 1.5.265
sections.original: Sed nunc peropus est , aut hunc cum ipsa , aut me aliquid de illa adversum hunc loqui .
sections.translation: But now there ’ s an absolute necessity , either for him to speak to her , or for me to speak to him about her . While the mind is in suspense , it is swayed by a slight impulse one way or the other .

sections.label: Section 1.5.267
sections.original: Quis hic loquitur ? Mysis ? salve .
sections.translation: Who is it speaking here ? ( Seeing her .) Mysis ? Good-morrow to you .

sections.label: Section 1.5.267b
sections.original: O salve , Pamphile .
sections.translation: Oh ! Good-morrow to you , Pamphilus .

sections.label: Section 1.5.267c
sections.original: Quid agit ?
sections.translation: How is she ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.267d
sections.original: Rogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask ? She is oppressed with grief , [*] and on this account the poor thing is anxious , because some time ago the marriage was arranged for this day . Then , too , she fears this ,

sections.label: Section 1.5.270
sections.original: Ne deseras se .
sections.translation: that you may forsake her .

sections.label: Section 1.5.270b
sections.original: Hem , egone istuc conari queam ?
sections.translation: Ha ! could I attempt that ? Could I suffer her , poor thing , to be deceived on my account ? She , who has confided to me her affection , and her entire existence ? She , whom I have held especially dear to my feelings as my wife ? Shall I suffer her mind , well and chastely trained and tutored ,

sections.label: Section 1.5.275
sections.original: Coactum egestate ingenium immutarier ?
sections.translation: to be overcome by poverty and corrupted ? I will not do it .

sections.label: Section 1.5.276b
sections.original: Haud vereor si in te solo sit situm ;
sections.translation: I should have no fear if it rested with yourself alone ; but whether you may be able to withstand compulsion —

sections.label: Section 1.5.277b
sections.original: Adeone me ignavum putas ,
sections.translation: Do you deem me so cowardly , so utterly ungrateful , inhuman , and so brutish , that neither intimacy , nor affection , nor shame ,

sections.label: Section 1.5.280
sections.original: Commoveat neque commoneat ut servem fidem ?
sections.translation: can move or admonish me to keep faith ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.281
sections.original: Unum hoc scio , esse meritam ut memor esses sui .
sections.translation: This one thing I know , that she is deserving that you should not forget her .

sections.label: Section 1.5.282
sections.original: Memor essem ? o Mysis Mysis , etiam nunc mihi
sections.translation: Forget her ? Oh Mysis , Mysis , at this moment are those words of Chrysis concerning Glycerium written on my mind . Now at the point of death , she called me ;

sections.label: Line 285
sections.original: Accessi : vos semotae : nos soli : incipit :
sections.translation: I went to her ; you had withdrawn ; we were alone ; she began : My dear Pamphilus , you see her beauty and her youth ; and it is not unknown to you to what extent both of these are now of use to her , in protecting both her chastity and her interests . By this right hand I do entreat you , and by your good Genius , [*]

sections.label: Section 1.5.290
sections.original: Per tuam fidem , perque huius solitudinem ,
sections.translation: by your own fidelity , and by her bereft condition , do not withdraw yourself from her , or forsake her ; if I have loved you as my own brother , or if she has always prized you above all others , or has been obedient to you in all things .

sections.label: Section 1.5.295
sections.original: Te isti virum do amicum tutorem patrem .
sections.translation: You do I give to her as a husband , friend , protector , father . This property of mine do I intrust to you , and commit to your care . She placed her in my hands ; that instant , death came upon her . I accepted her ; having accepted , I will protect her .

sections.label: Section 1.5.298b
sections.original: Ita spero quidem .
sections.translation: So indeed I hope .

sections.label: Section 1.5.299
sections.original: Sed cur tu abis ab illa ?
sections.translation: But why are you leaving her ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.299b
sections.original: Obstetricem arcesso .
sections.translation: I ’ m going to fetch the midwife . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.5.299c
sections.original: Propera , atque audin ’?
sections.translation: Make all haste . And — do you hear ?—

sections.label: Section 1.5.300
sections.original: Verbum unum cave de nuptiis , ne ad morbum hoc etiam .
sections.translation: take care , and not one word about the marriage , lest that too should add to her illness .

sections.label: Section 1.5.300b
sections.original: Teneo .
sections.translation: I understand .

sections.label: Section 2.1.301
sections.original: Quid ais Byrrhia ? daturne illa Pamphilo hodie nuptum ?
sections.translation: How say you , Byrrhia ? Is she to be given in marriage to Pamphilus to-day ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.301b
sections.original: Sic est .
sections.translation: It is so .

sections.label: Section 2.1.302
sections.original: Qui scis ?
sections.translation: How do you know ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.302b
sections.original: Apud forum modo e Davo audivi .
sections.translation: I heard it just now from Davus at the Forum .

sections.label: Section 2.1.302c
sections.original: Vae misero mihi .
sections.translation: Woe unto wretched me ! As , hitherto , until now , my mind has been racked amid hope and fear ; so , since hope has been withdrawn , wearied with care , it sinks overwhelmed .

sections.label: Section 2.1.305
sections.original: Quaeso edepol , Charine , quoniam non potest id fieri quod vis ,
sections.translation: By my troth , Charinus , since that which you wish can not come to pass , prithee , do wish that which can .

sections.label: Section 2.1.306b
sections.original: Nil volo aliud nisi Philumenam .
sections.translation: I wish for nothing else but Philumena .

sections.label: Section 2.1.306c
sections.original: Ah ,
sections.translation: Alas ! How much better were it for you to endeavor to expel that passion from your mind , than to be saying that by which your desire is to no purpose still more inflamed .

sections.label: Section 2.1.309
sections.original: Facile omnes cum valemus recta consilia aegrotis damus .
sections.translation: We all , when we are well , with ease give good advice to the sick . If you were in my situation , you would think otherwise .

sections.label: Section 2.1.310b
sections.original: Age age , ut libet .
sections.translation: Well , well , just as you like .

sections.label: Section 2.1.310c
sections.original: Sed Pamphilum
sections.translation: But I see Pamphilus ; I ’ m determined I ’ ll try every thing before I despair .

sections.label: Section 2.1.311b
sections.original: Quid hic agit ?
sections.translation: What does he mean ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.312
sections.original: Ipsum hunc orabo : huic supplicabo : amorem huic narrabo meum :
sections.translation: I will entreat his own self ; I will supplicate him ; I will disclose to him my love . I think that I shall prevail upon him to put off the marriage for some days at least ; in the mean time , something will turn up , I trust .

sections.label: Section 2.1.314b
sections.original: Id aliquid nihil est .
sections.translation: That something is nothing .

sections.label: Section 2.1.314c
sections.original: Byrrhia ,
sections.translation: Byrrhia ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.315
sections.original: Quid tibi videtur ? adeon ’ ad eum ?
sections.translation: how seems it to you ? Shall I accost him ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.315b
sections.original: Quidni ? Si nihil impetres ,
sections.translation: Why not ? Should you not prevail , that at least he may look upon you as a gallant ready provided for him , if he marries her .

sections.label: Section 2.1.317
sections.original: Abin ’ hinc in malam rem cum suspicione istac , scelus ?
sections.translation: Away with you to perdition with that vile suggestion , you rascal !

sections.label: Section 2.1.318
sections.original: Charinum video : salve .
sections.translation: I espy Charinus . ( Accosting him .) Good-morrow !

sections.label: Section 2.1.318b
sections.original: O salve Pamphile .
sections.translation: O , good-morrow . Pamphilus , I ’ m come to you , seeking hope , safety , counsel , and assistance .

sections.label: Section 2.1.320
sections.original: Neque pol consili locum habeo , neque auxili copiam .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I have neither time for counsel , nor resources for assistance . But what ’ s the matter now ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.321b
sections.original: Hodie uxorem ducis ?
sections.translation: To-day you are going to take a wife ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.321c
sections.original: Aiunt .
sections.translation: So they say .

sections.label: Section 2.1.321d
sections.original: Pamphile ,
sections.translation: Pamphilus , if you do that , you behold me this day for the last time .
